beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Salut, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes -- BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: The model selection passed. MobileNetV2-0.5, 89.8% accuracy on the defect-detection task, compiled for the STM32H7. Then the numbers came back: 4 megabytes of flash, 1.2 megabytes of SRAM, 320 milliseconds per inference. The ceilings were 2 megabytes, 1 megabyte, 250 milliseconds. It missed on every axis. Three knobs can close that gap -- quantization, pruning, distillation. The question is in what order, and whether the combination ships.
beats.visual_intent: title card: STM32H7 target with 3 ceilings; FP32 misses all three; the three-knob problem

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude -- write compression_journey dot py, MobileNetV2-0.5 on STM32H7 with 2 MB flash, 1 MB SRAM, 250 ms. Steps: FP32 to int8 PTQ, then add 25% structured prune, then add distillation. Track flash, SRAM, latency, and accuracy against ceilings. Print a four-state table.

props.command:
claude "write compression_journey.py:
  MobileNetV2-0.5 on STM32H7
  (2MB flash, 1MB SRAM, 250ms)
  Steps: fp32 -> int8 PTQ -> +25% prune
  -> +distill. Track flash/SRAM/latency/
  accuracy vs ceilings, print 4-state table."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. INT8 cuts weights from 4 bytes to 1, so flash drops from 4 MB to 1 MB. Pruning removes 25% of channels, shrinking flash further to 0.75 MB and latency to 240 ms. Distillation adds back 0.8% accuracy with no size change. The order matters: quantize first, then prune the quantized network.

props.code:
# compression_journey.py  --  Claude Code output
STATES = [
  {"name": "FP32",
   "flash_mb": 4.0, "sram_mb": 1.2,
   "latency_ms": 320, "acc": 89.8},
  {"name": "INT8 PTQ",
   "flash_mb": 1.0, "sram_mb": 0.8,
   "latency_ms": 320, "acc": 89.8},
  {"name": "+PRUNE 25%",
   "flash_mb": 0.75, "sram_mb": 0.6,
   "latency_ms": 240, "acc": 89.0},
  {"name": "+DISTILL",
   "flash_mb": 0.75, "sram_mb": 0.6,
   "latency_ms": 240, "acc": 90.6},
]
CEILS = {"flash": 2.0, "sram": 1.0, "lat": 250, "acc": 88.0}
for s in STATES:
    fits = (s["flash_mb"] <= CEILS["flash"] and
            s["sram_mb"]  <= CEILS["sram"]  and
            s["latency_ms"] <= CEILS["lat"])
    print(f"{s['name']:12} flash={s['flash_mb']}MB SRAM={s['sram_mb']}MB "
          f"lat={s['latency_ms']}ms acc={s['acc']}%  SHIP={fits}")

beats.narration_text: Run it. Four states, four metric columns. FP32 blows all three ceilings -- flash in red, SRAM in red, latency in red. INT8 drops flash from 4 to 1 megabyte and SRAM to 0.8. Latency stays at 320 -- quantization doesn't move the compute bottleneck. Add pruning: flash falls to 0.75, latency finally drops to 240, under the ceiling. Add distillation: accuracy climbs 1.6 points, from 89.0 to 90.6, above where we started. That's the ship state.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: 4 grouped bars per metric (FLASH, SRAM, LATENCY, ACCURACY) across 4 states; ceiling lines; FP32 bars in CRIMSON (over), subsequent states in INK (under)

beats.narration_text: Now check what happens if you do it in the wrong order. Iterate: claude -- add a fifth column showing quantize-before-prune -- the order-matters accuracy penalty.

props.command:
claude "add a 5th column: what happens if you
  quantize BEFORE pruning -- show the
  order-matters accuracy penalty"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. Two bars now: quantize then prune versus prune then quantize. Quantize-before-prune accuracy in crimson -- 88.1%, barely above the ceiling. Prune-before-quantize in ink -- 90.6%, a 2.5 point spread from sequencing alone. Same operations, same model, different order.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: two-bar comparison showing quantize->prune (CRIMSON, lower acc 88.1%) vs prune->quantize (INK, higher acc 90.6%); order-matters label

beats.narration_text: So: pruning earned the fit, distillation earned the margin, quantization made it possible -- and doing them out of order compounds error. The ship state is 0.75 MB flash, 0.6 MB SRAM, 240 ms, 90.6% accuracy. Three knobs, applied in the right sequence.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: three-knob sequence (quantize -> prune -> distill) with ship state metrics; order matters warning

beats.narration_text: Your move: take your own model's parameter count and run the four-state table against your target's ceilings. If pruning alone doesn't close the gap, add distillation against the float teacher. And check the order before you run -- 2.5 accuracy points is not a rounding error.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run your own param count through the table, 2) add distillation if pruning falls short, 3) always prune before quantize

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. So: pruning earned the fit, distillation earned the margin, quantization made it possible -- and doing them out of order compounds error. The ship state is 0.75 MB flash, 0.6 MB SRAM, 240 ms, 90.6% accuracy. Three knobs, applied in the right sequence. Your move: take your own model's parameter count and run the four-state table against your target's ceilings. If pruning alone doesn't close the gap, add distillation against the float teacher. And check the order before you run -- 2.5 accuracy points is not a rounding error.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [The MobileNetV2-0.5 That Almost Fit] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.title: The MobileNetV2-0.5 That Almost Fit
props.subline: and check the order before you run -- 2.5 accuracy points is not a rounding erro
